bugs.description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning based on the remaining depth. For the worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the branching factor becomes huge (up to ~200 candidates per level) and the recursion explores an exponential number of states. This leads to a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases, even though the problem size is small.
bugs.actual_behavior: The program does not finish within the typical time limits (it runs for many seconds and eventually times out), producing no output.

bugs.description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning based on the remaining depth. For the worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the branching factor becomes huge (up to ~200 candidates per level) and the recursion explores an exponential number of states. This leads to a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases, even though the problem size is small.
bugs.actual_behavior: The program does not finish within the typical time limits (it runs for many seconds and eventually times out), producing no output.

bugs.bug_description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning based on the remaining depth. For the worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the branching factor becomes huge (up to ~200 candidates per level) and the recursion explores an exponential number of states. This leads to a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases, even though the problem size is small.
bugs.reasoning: The search starts with a single element {0} and at each recursion step adds a new element chosen from the set of all x±a_i for every x in the current b and every a_i. With n = 10, the size of b can grow to 10, so the candidate set can contain up to 2·|b|·n ≤ 200 elements. The recursion depth is n‑1 = 9, giving a theoretical branching factor of up to 200 and a worst‑case number of explored states on the order of 200^9, which is astronomically large. The code has no memoization, pruning, or early termination except when all a_i are already covered, and for inputs that have no solution (as in the provided triggering example) the covered condition never becomes true early. Consequently the program will explore an exponential search space and will not finish within typical time limits, confirming the reported TLE bug.

evaluations.bug_description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning based on the remaining depth. For the worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the branching factor becomes huge (up to ~200 candidates per level) and the recursion explores an exponential number of states. This leads to a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases, even though the problem size is small.
evaluations.reasoning: The search starts with a single element {0} and at each recursion step adds a new element chosen from the set of all x±a_i for every x in the current b and every a_i. With n = 10, the size of b can grow to 10, so the candidate set can contain up to 2·|b|·n ≤ 200 elements. The recursion depth is n‑1 = 9, giving a theoretical branching factor of up to 200 and a worst‑case number of explored states on the order of 200^9, which is astronomically large. The code has no memoization, pruning, or early termination except when all a_i are already covered, and for inputs that have no solution (as in the provided triggering example) the covered condition never becomes true early. Consequently the program will explore an exponential search space and will not finish within typical time limits, confirming the reported TLE bug.

bugs.description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the candidate set can contain up to ~200 elements per recursion level, leading to an exponential search space (≈200⁹ states). This causes a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases.
bugs.fix: Replace the naïve DFS with a bounded backtracking that assigns each a_i a coefficient of -1, 0, or 1 (meaning a_i is used as b_j‑b_k, ignored, or the opposite) and checks whether a non‑trivial linear combination sums to zero. Because n ≤ 10, a simple recursive search with memoization on (position, current_sum) or a DP over possible sums (using a set) runs in O(3ⁿ) ≈ 59049 operations, well within limits. The algorithm should also early‑exit when a zero sum is achieved with at least one non‑zero coefficient.
bugs.reasoning: The defender’s evaluation confirms that the current implementation explores an astronomically large state space and does not terminate within typical time limits for inputs such as the provided triggering case. The lack of memoization or pruning makes the exponential blow‑up unavoidable, confirming the reported TLE bug.

final_bugs.description: The algorithm performs an exhaustive depth‑first search without any memoization or pruning. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the candidate set can contain up to ~200 elements per recursion level, leading to an exponential search space (≈200⁹ states). This causes a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases.
final_bugs.fix: Replace the naïve DFS with a bounded backtracking that assigns each a_i a coefficient of -1, 0, or 1 (meaning a_i is used as b_j‑b_k, ignored, or the opposite) and checks whether a non‑trivial linear combination sums to zero. Because n ≤ 10, a simple recursive search with memoization on (position, current_sum) or a DP over possible sums (using a set) runs in O(3ⁿ) ≈ 59049 operations, well within limits. The algorithm should also early‑exit when a zero sum is achieved with at least one non‑zero coefficient.
final_bugs.reasoning: The defender’s evaluation confirms that the current implementation explores an astronomically large state space and does not terminate within typical time limits for inputs such as the provided triggering case. The lack of memoization or pruning makes the exponential blow‑up unavoidable, confirming the reported TLE bug.

For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n = 10 with many distinct large values) the candidate set can contain up to ~200 elements per recursion level, leading to an exponential search space (≈200⁹ states). This causes a time‑limit exceeded (TLE) on legitimate test cases.
aggregated_bugs.fix: Replace the naïve DFS with a bounded backtracking that assigns each a_i a coefficient of -1, 0, or 1 (meaning a_i is used as b_j‑b_k, ignored, or the opposite) and checks whether a non‑trivial linear combination sums to zero. Because n ≤ 10, a simple recursive search with memoization on (position, current_sum) or a DP over possible sums (using a set) runs in O(3ⁿ) ≈ 59049 operations, well within limits. The algorithm should also early‑exit when a zero sum is achieved with at least one non‑zero coefficient.

The lack of memoization or pruning makes the exponential blow‑up unavoidable, confirming the reported TLE bug.
